summary: Gruber examines different approaches to presenting notes on the web, comparing sidenotes (margin notes) and footnotes. He highlights two well-done CSS-based sidenote implementations while explaining why he chose footnotes for Daring Fireball. He argues that web designers should learn from centuries of print typography rather than defaulting to pop-ups and tooltips. He also responds to Joe Clark's critique of his footnote design, agreeing that HTML lacks proper semantic markup for notes, but pushing back on Clark's claim that his back-arrow glyph choice was wrong.
key_points: Sidenotes eliminate the problem of helping readers connect notes to their referenced text, but break down when notes are long or closely spaced
Both footnotes and sidenotes are superior to pop-up and tooltip-style inline notes, drawing on hundreds of years of proven print design
HTML's lack of semantic markup specifically for footnotes and sidenotes is a glaring omission that standards bodies have neglected
Unicode characters like ↩ and ★ offer a useful middle ground between punctuation and iconography, but poor Windows rendering has limited their adoption on the web
The left-pointing hooked arrow ↩ means 'back' in web convention (dating to Mosaic), not 'move left' as Clark argued
money_quotes: No, the web is not print, but that doesn't mean web designers can't build upon and learn from traditional typography and print design.
Both footnotes and sidenotes are well-represented in good design from the past few hundred years.
That there exists no HTML tag syntax specifically appropriate for notes is a glaring omission, and but one that standards wonks don't seem to care about.
I love using Unicode characters for purposes such as this — it feels like a cross between punctuation and iconography.
The only reason these characters exist in Unicode is that they're supposed to be, well, used.
I chose '↩' not because it implies moving left, but because it implies moving back. And a left-pointing arrow has meant 'back' ever since Mosaic.
key_insight: Good web typography should build on centuries of print design convention rather than reinventing annotation from scratch, and the web's failure to provide semantic markup for notes remains a significant oversight.
